one_line_takeaway: CompassDPO reframes DPO robustness as a training-dynamics problem (gradient dominance from a small subset of high-influence preference pairs) and fixes it with reward-free, margin-derived directional mixing plus adaptive soft winsorization, consistently beating vanilla DPO and DPO-family robustness baselines (IPO/cDPO/rDPO/Dr.DPO) on safety Win Rate and Attack Success Rate across four backbones, in OOD generalization, and under synthetic label-flip corruption.
key_finding: On PKU-SafeRLHF-30K in-distribution (Table 1), CompassDPO improves over vanilla DPO and all DPO-family baselines across all four backbones, e.g. on Pythia-2.8B: WR 59.92% vs DPO's 44.23% and Dr.DPO's 57.41%, MD-ASR 13.03% vs DPO's 57.14% and Dr.DPO's 17.04%; on Qwen2.5-7B: WR 90.23% vs DPO's 75.81%, MD-ASR 1.63% vs DPO's 20.55%, NV-ASR 0.00%. On OOD benchmarks (Table 2), for Llama-3-8B CompassDPO attains the lowest average ASR among DPO-family methods (AVG 1.95 vs Dr.DPO 2.09, rDPO 3.97, DPO 22.85); for Qwen2.5-7B, AVG ASR 0.64 vs Dr.DPO 1.05, rDPO 3.18, DPO 14.39. Under synthetic label-flip noise on Pythia-2.8B (Table 3), CompassDPO degrades most gracefully: at 30% flips WR 55.14% (vs Dr.DPO 52.93%, DPO 41.82%) and MD-ASR 22.06% (vs Dr.DPO 31.95%, DPO 69.05%). The ablation (Fig. 4) shows magnitude control alone yields larger gains than directional control alone, and the two are complementary, with the combined method performing best. Hyperparameter sensitivity analysis shows stable performance over broad ranges of rho_f (0.05-0.4, final=0.3), rho_w^max (final=0.5), and q (final=0.7), with degradation only at extreme settings.
core_contribution: The paper (titled in the actual PDF 'CompassDPO: Dynamics-Controlled Direct Preference Optimization for Robust Safety Alignment', arXiv:2603.07211v2) diagnoses a batch-level failure mode of vanilla DPO called 'gradient dominance', where a small subset of high-influence preference pairs comes to dominate the gradient energy of mini-batch updates as training proceeds (rising tail-energy share and Herfindahl-Hirschman Index concentration, measured on Pythia-2.8B), while validation margin/preference accuracy stagnate. The paper proposes CompassDPO, a reward-free method that regulates each mini-batch update along two axes computed purely from signals already available during standard DPO training (the implicit DPO margin): (1) directional control - after a warm-up period (t < 0.3T), it computes a reversed-direction loss for each pair, applies sparsemax to retain only a sparse set of high-gain pairs, and mixes their loss with the reversed-direction loss under a batch-level mixing budget rho_f, to attenuate updates that conflict with the emerging preference direction; (2) magnitude control - it identifies the top loss-quantile (q) tail of a batch's mixed losses and adaptively soft-winsorizes (caps) their contribution toward the quantile threshold, with the cap strength ρw computed adaptively from batch margin statistics (not a fixed constant) rather than discarding hard examples outright. No external reward model, relabeling, or data reconstruction is used.
threat_model: The paper's threat model is data-quality/label-noise robustness, not an adversarial attacker manipulating the model at inference time. Specifically: (1) naturally occurring 'imperfect preference supervision' in real-world human preference datasets (noisy/inconsistent annotator labels), and (2) a controlled adversarial-style stress test where a fixed fraction (10/20/30%) of training preference labels in PKU-SafeRLHF-30K are synthetically flipped to simulate corrupted/poisoned supervision. Downstream safety is evaluated via Attack Success Rate (ASR) on jailbreak/harmful-instruction benchmarks (HarmBench, Salad-Bench) using two independent safety judge models, i.e. robustness of the resulting aligned policy to adversarial prompts is the evaluation target, but the paper's own intervention operates purely on the training-time optimization dynamics, not on inference-time defenses.
limitations: Stated by the authors: experiments focus mainly on safety alignment with PKU-SafeRLHF plus external safety benchmarks and one helpfulness-oriented setting; broader preference-learning domains and languages are untested. The method relies on batch-level margin and loss-tail statistics, so very small batch sizes may give less reliable estimates, and directional control requires a warm-up period since early margin signals are unstable. CompassDPO does not specifically target over-refusal behavior. Additional limitations evident from the paper: the diagnostic gradient-dominance analysis (Fig. 2) is shown only on a single backbone (Pythia-2.8B), so it is unclear whether the same batch-level dynamics generalize to the larger/other backbones used elsewhere in the paper; the reference DPO run in that diagnostic uses 3 epochs specifically to expose dominance, an asymmetric setup versus CompassDPO's 1-epoch default, which is acknowledged but complicates a fully apples-to-apples dynamics comparison; safety judges (MD-Judge, Nemotron Safety Guard) and the GPT-4.1-mini WR judge are themselves imperfect classifiers whose own error/bias could affect reported numbers.

datasets_used: PKU-SafeRLHF-30K (26,874 train / 2,989 test pairs; 798 unique deduplicated prompts used for ASR eval)
Do-Not-Answer (938 test prompts, OOD safety benchmark)
HarmBench (200 test prompts, OOD safety benchmark)
HH-RLHF (8,273 test prompts, OOD safety benchmark, red-teaming split)
MMLU-Pro, MT-Bench, OR-Bench (OR-Hard-1K, OR-Toxic) — auxiliary general-capability/over-refusal checks reported in appendix
quantitative_results: PKU-SafeRLHF-30K Pythia-2.8B: CompassDPO WR 59.92% / MD-ASR 13.03% / NV-ASR 3.01% vs DPO WR 44.23% / MD-ASR 57.14% / NV-ASR 19.17% vs Dr.DPO WR 57.41% / MD-ASR 17.04% / NV-ASR 4.14%
PKU-SafeRLHF-30K Qwen2.5-7B: CompassDPO WR 90.23% / MD-ASR 1.63% / NV-ASR 0.00% vs DPO WR 75.81% / MD-ASR 20.55% / NV-ASR 7.27%
OOD average ASR, Llama-3-8B: CompassDPO 1.95 vs Dr.DPO 2.09 vs rDPO 3.97 vs DPO 22.85
OOD average ASR, Qwen2.5-7B: CompassDPO 0.64 vs Dr.DPO 1.05 vs rDPO 3.18 vs DPO 14.39
Label-flip robustness (Pythia-2.8B, 30% flip): CompassDPO WR 55.14% / MD-ASR 22.06% vs Dr.DPO WR 52.93% / MD-ASR 31.95% vs DPO WR 41.82% / MD-ASR 69.05%
Default hyperparameters: warm-up ratio alpha=0.3, directional budget rho_f=0.3, magnitude cap strength rho_w^max=0.5, tail quantile q=0.7
